data.title: Nifty Realty snaps 6-day losing streak, rises 1% on value-buying; Raymond Realty, DLF among top gainers
data.description:
Nifty Realty index ended a six-session losing streak on Friday, rising nearly 1 percent on value buying in select counters, with Raymond Realty, Brigade Enterprises and DLF among the top performers.

The Nifty Realty index climbed 0.97 percent during the trade, recovering from a nearly 6 percent fall in the previous six sessions.

Raymond Realty led the gains, hitting the 5 percent upper circuit to Rs 926.20 on the NSE. The company was recently listed following its demerger from Raymond Ltd as part of a restructuring exercise aimed at unlocking value.

On July 2, Negen Capital Services Pvt Ltd acquired 3.89 lakh shares, or 0.58 percent stake, in Raymond Realty via a bulk deal at an average price of Rs 921.12 apiece.

Brigade Enterprises and The Phoenix Mills also advanced 1.88 percent and 1.82 percent, respectively. Godrej Properties and DLF gained up to 0.5 percent.

Meanwhile, the headline indices opened higher amid firm global cues and buying in bank stocks, but gains were capped as investors remained cautious ahead of India’s expected trade agreement with the United States.

The Sensex rose 67.34 points to 83,306.81 in early trade, while the Nifty edged up 23.55 points to 25,428.85. However, both indices later turned range-bound, with the Sensex slipping 13.55 points to 83,221.65 and the Nifty down 4.15 points at 25,400.40.Disclaimer: The views and investment tips expressed by experts on Moneycontrol are their own and not those of the website or its management. Moneycontrol advises users to check with certified experts before taking any investment decisions.

data.title: India's PC Jeweller surges on robust June quarter sales
data.description:
** Shares of PC Jeweller up 15.8% to 16.2 rupees

** Co says June-qtr standalone rev grew 80% y/y on high demand due to wedding or festive purchases

** Co reduces outstanding debt by ~7.5% in June-qtr, targeting to be debt free by end of FY26

** Stock set to rise most in a session since Dec 19, 2023; on track to rise for fourth straight session

** More than 314 mln shares traded, ~15x its 30-day moving avg

** Shares traded most since Jan 2, 2024

** PCJE up 3% YTD

data.title: UK Construction Output Declines Slightly in June
data.description:
The S&P Global UK Construction PMI rose to 48.8 in June 2025 from 47.9 in May, still below the 50.0 threshold that separates growth from contraction.

Residential building returned to modest growth (50.7), but this was outweighed by steeper declines in commercial (45.1) and civil engineering (44.2) activity.

New orders fell for the sixth straight month, with firms citing weak demand, fewer tenders, and cautious client spending.

Business confidence dropped to its lowest level since December 2022.

Employment continued to decline amid cost-cutting efforts.

Purchasing activity fell, though at the slowest rate in five months, and supplier performance improved.

Input cost inflation persisted but eased to its softest level since January.

data.title: UK New Car Sales Rise 6.7% in June
data.description:
UK new car sales rose 6.7% year-on-year in June 2025 to 191,316 units, marking a second straight month of growth and the strongest June performance since 2019, driven mainly by fleet demand, which surged 8.5% to 114,841 units.

Private retail sales grew 5.9% to 71,616 units but accounted for just 37.4% of the market—less than four in ten new cars.

Business registrations declined by 15.8% to 4,859 units.

Petrol registrations fell 4.2% and diesel remained flat (+0.2%), with their combined share now at 51.6%.

Electrified vehicles gained ground, reaching 92,571 units and a 48.5% market share.

Plug-in models led growth: battery electric vehicles (BEVs) jumped 39.1% to 47,354 units (24.8% of the market), while plug-in hybrids (PHEVs) rose 28.8% to 21,382 units.

In contrast, hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs) saw an 8.5% drop to 23,835 units.

data.title: Guy Carp launches London poaching suit against Willis Re
data.description:
(The Insurer) – Guy Carpenter has launched legal action against Willis Re and several senior Willis Group executives over the recent attempted mass raid on its London and Bermuda operations.

  • Guy Carp and Marsh file in London after Willis Re June team raids

  • Suit names former GC specialties exec Summers and Bermuda head Fletcher

  • WTW president of Risk & Broking Lucy Clarke also named

  • WTW’s Bain Capital-backed reinsurance JV unveiled in December

The claim was filed at London’s High Court on July 3. Guy Carpenter and its sister Marsh McLennan-owned broker Marsh are sueing rival Willis’ new start-up reinsurance broker alongside seven other named defendants.

The lawsuit names Guy Carpenter and Company, Marsh Services, Marsh and Guy Carpenter Bermuda as claimants and also targets Martino UK Operating Company and Martino Holdings alongside Willis, Willis Group and Willis Re Bermuda, according to information from legal data provider Solomonic.

UK’s Companies House disclosures show Martino Holdings to have been incorporated in September 2024 as a private limited company involved in “non-life reinsurance”. Directors of the business include WTW president of Risk & Broking Lucy Clake and Bain Capital Partner and Global Head of Insurance Matt Popoli.

WTW confirmed in December that it was re-entering treaty reinsurance broking through a joint venture with private equity house Bain Capital.

The London legal claim, filed with the King’s Bench division of the UK’s commercial court, also lists Clarke, Guy Carpenter’s deputy CEO of global specialties James (Jim) Summers and Guy Carpenter Bermuda CEO John Fletcher as named defendants.

The Insurer was first to report in early June that Summers had resigned alongside at least five senior members of his team to join Willis Re, in a raid which fired the starting gun for further team moves.

This publication later revealed that WTW’s new Bain-backed reinsurance venture had attempted to raid Guy Carpenter’s Bermuda office to hire senior team members including CEO Fletcher.

The moves prompted Guy Carpenter to issue a statement on June 12 in which it said it would “vigorously pursue" its rights and consider legal action in response to the departure of senior staff members.

The particulars of the claim have not yet been filed, and the specifics on which the legal action is based were not immediately clear.

The Guy Carpenter and Marsh claimants are represented by Stephenson Harwood LLP. Counsel information for Willis, Willis Re and the other named defendants was not immediately available.

Marsh, Guy Carpenter, Willis and Willis Re were contacted for comment.

data.description:

data.title: FlexQube Wins New SEK2 Million Order from German Automotive Manufacturer
data.description:
FlexQube received an order worth 2.4 million kronor for material handling carts for an unnamed German automotive manufacturer.

The cart-based material handling technology company said Friday the order will be delivered in the third quarter. FlexQube received the order through its new commercial partnership with Transpofix.

data.title: VRL Logistics Gets Board Nod for Bonus Issue of Shares
data.description:
VRL Logistics has received board approval for the issue of bonus shares in the ratio of 1:1, according to a Friday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

The plans to issue one new fully paid-up equity share of 10 rupees each for each existing fully paid-up equity share of 10 rupees to the eligible equity shareholders of the company

The bonus shares will be issued out of eligible reserves, including securities premium account, general reserve, capital redemption reserve, and retained earnings, available as of March 31.

The company's shares were up over 1% in recent trade.

data.title: Dollar slips versus major currencies as US tariff deadline looms
data.description:
Key points:
  • Trump's tax bill raises fiscal sustainability concerns
  • U.S. expected to specify tariff rates to countries soon
  • Japan said to send chief trade negotiator to U.S. amid tariff tensions

The dollar slipped against other major currencies on Friday after President Donald Trump got his signature tax cut bill over the final hurdle and pressure mounted on countries to secure trade deals with the United States.

The U.S. currency had rallied on Thursday after stronger than expected U.S. jobs data pushed out the timing for potential rate cuts by the Federal Reserve. But the dollar index, which tracks the currency against major peers, is headed for a second-straight weekly decline.

The Republican-controlled House of Representatives narrowly passed Trump's "One, Big, Beautiful Bill" of spending and tax cuts that is estimated to add $3.4 trillion to the country's $36.2 trillion debt. Trump is expected to sign the bill into law on Friday.

With the U.S. closed for Independence Day, attention turns to Trump's July 9 deadline when sweeping tariffs take effect on countries like Japan that have not yet secured trade agreements.

"The appetite for the dollar is waning because, one, the U.S. debt worries are rising and appetite for U.S. debt is at risk," said Ipek Ozkardeskaya, senior market analyst at Swissquote Bank.

"And also because of the fact that the tariff situation and trade disruptions are going to have a negative impact on growth for the U.S. and the Fed will not necessarily be able to support the economy when inflation risks are rising."

The dollar index had its worst first half since 1973 as Trump's chaotic roll-out of sweeping tariffs heightened concerns about the U.S. economy and the safety of Treasuries. The U.S. currency sank to the lowest in more than three years against the euro and British pound earlier in the week.

The dollar index edged 0.1% lower to 96.96, trimming its 0.4% advance on Thursday. The euro added 0.1% to $1.1773 , poised for a 0.4% weekly gain.

The yen climbed 0.4% to 144.375 versus the dollar, while the Swiss franc firmed 0.2% to fetch 0.7939 per dollar.

Trump said many countries will get letters on Friday specifying what tariff rates they will face, marking a shift from earlier pledges to do individual deals with trading partners.

European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen said the EU was aiming for a trade agreement "in principle" with the U.S. before the deadline. Japan, which has been a focus of Trump's ire of late, is reportedly sending its chief trade negotiator to the U.S. again as early as this weekend.

Adding to strains on global trade, China said it would implement duties of up to 34.9% on brandy originating in the European Union for a period of five years starting from July 5.

Investors worried about the health of the U.S. economy got some relief on Thursday via the U.S. Labor Department's employment report which showed that nonfarm payrolls increased by 147,000 jobs in June, well ahead of economists' forecast in a Reuters poll for a rise of 110,000.

"The U.S. labour market is gradually slowing down, but the fact that it hasn't experienced a sudden change is reassuring," said SMBC chief currency strategist Hirofumi Suzuki. "I personally predict that the tariff negotiations will not be very favourable, leading to continued dollar weakness and yen strength."

Market expectations that the Fed will leave rates unchanged at its July meeting are now at 95.3% probability, up from 76.2% on July 2, according to the CME's Fedwatch tool.

Economists continue to expect the Fed will not start cutting rates again until September or even later.

data.title: South Korean Shares Slip 2% on Trade Deal Uncertainty After President Lee's Tariff Remarks
data.description:
South Korean shares closed lower on Friday over uncertainty regarding a favorable trade deal with the US. The uncertainty was reflected in President Lee Jae-myung's statement, who said Thursday that it was uncertain whether Seoul and Washington would finalize tariff talks before the July 9 deadline.

The Korea Composite Stock Price Index or Kospi fell by 61.99 points, or 1.99%, to end at 3,054.28. The Kosdaq also decreased by 17.53 points, or 2.21%, to close at 775.8.

In economic news, South Korea's current account surplus increased to $10.14 billion in May from $5.70 billion in April, according to preliminary data from the Bank of Korea released Friday.

Compared with the year-ago period, the figure rose from a surplus of $9.09 billion, the central bank said.

The goods account registered a surplus of $10.66 billion. Exports fell by 2.9% on an annual basis to $56.93 billion while imports fell by 7.2% to $46.27 billion.

Meanwhile, the services account posted a deficit of $2.28 billion, primarily due to deficits in travel and other business services accounts.

In corporate news, Samsung Electronics launched its "2025 Bespoke AI One Body" washer-dryer on Friday, offering new AI features and a 7-inch AI Home touchscreen, the electronics products maker said in a Friday release.

The combined unit includes a 25kg washing machine and 22kg dryer, ideal for large laundry loads. It also features AI-powered functions for customized washing and drying, noise reduction, and automatic detergent injection.

The company claims that the redesigned inlet improves loading, while the unit cuts energy usage by 45% per kilogram. The model is priced between 3.7 million and 4.3 million won.

Shares of Samsung Electronics fell nearly 1% at market close.

data.title: Hang Seng Retreats 1.5% Weekly
data.description:
The Hang Seng lost 154 points or 0.6% to close at 23,916 on Friday, marking a second straight session of losses amid broad-based sector declines.

The benchmark dropped 1.5% for the week, reversing gains from the prior period as investors grew cautious ahead of U.S. President Trump’s July 9 tariff deadline.

Adding pressure, China’s commerce ministry finalized its EU brandy investigation, imposing tariffs of up to 34.9% for five years starting July 5.

Market participants also eyed key Chinese data due next week—including June CPI and PPI—amid lingering deflation risks.

U.S. markets were closed for Independence Day after record highs in the S&P 500 and Nasdaq, buoyed by strong jobs data.

Losses in Hong Kong were partially offset by optimism over possible stimulus at China’s upcoming Politburo meeting and news that the U.S. would ease export curbs on chip design software to China.

Major decliners included AIA Group (-4.6%), Techtronic Industries (-2.9%), and China Overseas Land (-2.3%).

data.title: Greece battles wildfire on Crete for a third day as temperatures rise
data.description:
Key points:
  • Firefighters battling Crete wildfire for a third day
  • Thousands of tourists left the area
  • Europe sweltering in early summer heatwave
  • Twenty cities in Italy placed on heat red alert

Firefighters battled for a third day on Friday to put out a wildfire on the Greek island of Crete that has razed forests and olive groves and forced thousands of residents and tourists to leave the area.

Around 130 firefighters, 48 vehicles and six helicopters were deployed in the effort, working with gale-force wind gusts and dry conditions that could rekindle the fire in areas already contained.

Wind gusts could fan embers from smouldering trunks of olive and pine trees on the ground, a fire brigade official told Reuters, speaking on condition of anonymity.

The fire in Crete comes as much of Europe swelters in an early summer heatwave, which officials have linked to at least eight deaths on the continent.

The fire, which broke out in a village about 16 km (10 miles) east of Ierapetra on Wednesday, has consumed swathes of agricultural land in the southeastern corner of the island, leaving dead animals, damaged houses and scorched farmhouses.

Some 1,000 residents evacuated on Wednesday found temporary shelter at an indoor stadium and in nearby hotels and about 5,000 holidaymakers left the area.

Many of the evacuees were expected to make their way back home later on Friday as the situation improved, George Tsapakos, a deputy civil protection governor for Crete, told Reuters.

Tourism is a key earner in Crete, the largest island in Greece, and local hoteliers were concerned about the impact on future bookings as the fire hit at the start of the peak summer holiday season.

Separately, some 148 firefighters battled a fire that broke out in the Athens suburb of Pikermi on Thursday, threatening many homes, causing power cuts and prompting authorities to move more than 300 people to safety. The fire was contained but not extinguished, the fire brigade official said.

Temperatures in Greece were forecast to reach up to 38 degrees Celsius (100.4 Fahrenheit) on Friday, the Greek weather service said.

In Italy, the health ministry put 20 of the 27 cities it monitors for heatwaves on red alert on Friday. RAI public broadcaster said temperatures would go as high as 38 C in Florence and 37 C in Rome, Bologna and Perugia.

With the heat comes a higher risk of wildfires.

Greece and other Mediterranean countries are in an area dubbed "a wildfire hotspot" by scientists - with blazes common during hot and dry summers. These have become more destructive in recent years due to a fast-changing climate.

data.title: Up-shine Lighting Buys Huizhou Hainankaimin Technology for 66 Million Yuan
data.description:
Up-shine Lighting subsidiary Huizhou Minbao Optoelectronics Technology fully acquired Huizhou Hainankaimin Technology for 66 million yuan.

The acquisition, including land and facilities, will support the company's industrial and special LED lighting project, according to a Friday filing with the Shenzhen bourse.

Shares of the commercial and architectural LED lighting products manufacturer closed 2% lower Friday.

data.title: Harbin Boshi Automation Signs Three-Year Packaging Line Operation, Maintenance Contract with CNPC
data.description:
Harbin Boshi Automation signed a solid product packaging line operation and maintenance contract with China National Petroleum Corp.'s Guangxi Petrochemical Branch, according to a Friday filing with the Shenzhen bourse.

CNPC's Guangxi Petrochemical Branch will operate and maintain Boshi Automations' solid product packaging lines and sales of spare parts for 109.1 million yuan for three years, the filing said.

data.title: Finnish police suspect 23-year-old man with criminal record in Tampere stabbing
data.description:
Finnish police said on Friday the person arrested on suspicion of stabbing and injuring four people near a shopping mall in Tampere on Thursday is a 23-year-old Finnish man with a previous criminal history.

Police told a press conference the suspect had told them he had no racist or political motive and that he had attacked people randomly.

Police said the injuries of the victims are serious.

data.title: Good Life Plus Plans Exit from Aquis Stock Exchange
data.description:
Good Life Plus (GDLF.AQ) plans to withdraw its shares from trading on the Aquis Stock Exchange Growth Market, saying a public listing no longer "meaningfully" benefits the company.

The luxury prize draw and rewards company said Friday it will seek shareholder approval for the plan at its annual general meeting on July 30. If approved, trading will cease on Aug. 4.
